Concepts agentic-constitution - A living .md file giving LLM agents high-level context about the user. Read before any task, updated weekly. atomic-notes - Note-taking p

title: Intentional Reading
text: Intentional Reading A deliberate approach to content consumption where reading time is structured into two phases: discovery (scanning for potential reads) and focused reading (highlighting, note-taking, connecting to existing knowledge). Described by YB (yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice) as a reaction to "cope consumption" - feeling productive while passively listening to podcasts or scrolling Substack. The core shift: every piece of content read should produce new atomic-notes and connections. Pipeline Curate sources - "Who gets access to my brain?" (references @phokarlsson's "Structure your milieu") Scroll = search - browsing time is for finding potential reads to add to a backlog Read with intent - dedicated time for highlighting and note-taking, even on short articles Extract to vault - Readwise plugin pulls highlights into Obsidian, user connects them to existing notes Counter-arguments Aggressive curation may create filter bubbles and miss valuable serendipitous exposure Treating every 5-minute read as a note-taking opportunity could make reading feel like work The "cope" framing may unfairly dismiss the value of passive, exploratory consumption Data gaps No specifics on YB's reading volume or how much note output this produces "Structure your milieu" concept not ingested as a source Single source describing this in an LLM-vault context

title: Context Compression
text: Context Compression Strategies for reducing the token cost of persistent context in LLM agent workflows. As projects accumulate context files (CLAUDE.md, memory, documentation), token consumption grows with every session. Context compression addresses this without sacrificing the benefits of compound-engineering. Two Approaches File-level compression Rewrite context files into more concise language while preserving semantic meaning. Caveman Compress achieves ~46% token reduction by compressing CLAUDE.md and memory files, preserving code blocks, URLs, and structural elements while condensing prose. Tradeoff: Compressed files are harder for humans to read and maintain. The compression itself requires an API call. Retrieval-level compression (progressive disclosure) Instead of loading all context at once, provide a layered retrieval system that surfaces only relevant context on demand. Claude-Mem implements a 3-layer system (search → timeline → full details) claiming ~10x token savings. Tradeoff: Requires running additional infrastructure (databases, HTTP services). Effectiveness depends on how selectively the agent actually retrieves. Why It Matters Token costs are a direct function of context size. For practitioners running multiple folder-as-agent projects with accumulated context, costs compound. A folder-agent with a rich CLAUDE.md, documentation, and operational memory might consume thousands of tokens just for startup context before any actual work begins. Context compres

title: LLM Wiki Pattern
text: LLM Wiki Pattern A knowledge management approach where an LLM agent reads raw sources and compiles them into structured, cross-referenced wiki pages. Distinguished from RAG by pre-compiling knowledge rather than re-deriving answers via vector search at query time. Originated by andrej-karpathy in a February 2025 tweet. At ~100 articles / ~400K words, Karpathy reported this approach outperforms RAG for personal Q&A. Key properties: Pre-compiled - knowledge is structured once, updated incrementally Compound loop - filed answers enrich future queries Bias-checked - counter-arguments and data gaps on every page Human-validated - gate prevents blind trust in AI output Research vs Personal Thinking YB (yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice) draws a distinction: the LLM wiki pattern is "fantastic for research projects" where synthetic artifacts make sense, but for personal thinking, "you can't outsource your own life experiences to AI." This suggests the pattern works best for external knowledge compilation, while personal knowledge management needs a complementary approach built on commonplace-practice and atomic-notes. Counter-arguments Requires more upfront processing than RAG May not scale as well to very large, rapidly changing corpora Single-user testimonial, not benchmarked rigorously Data gaps No head-to-head comparison with modern RAG systems (e.g., GraphRAG) Unclear at what scale the pre-compiled approach breaks down Original Karpathy tweet/thread not yet ingested as a prima

title: Keyboard-Driven Workflow
text: Keyboard-Driven Workflow A macOS productivity philosophy where the keyboard is the primary input device, minimizing mouse/trackpad usage through layered utilities. Described by Brett Terpstra as "the most powerful tool we have when using our Macs." The key insight is that the power comes from combining specialized tools into an ecosystem, not from any single utility. Terpstra's stack covers six categories: Navigation - screen text search (Superkey), Vim bindings in any app (KindaVim), fuzzy menu search (Paletro) Key modification - system-level remapping (Karabiner-Elements), Hyper key setup, DefaultKeyBindings.dict Launching - app/file launcher (LaunchBar), static key sequences (LeaderKey) Text expansion - snippet-based (TextExpander), AI-predictive (Cotypist) Window management - keyboard-driven positioning (Moom) Web browsing - Vim navigation (Vimium), keyboard-first search (Kagi) Overlaps with the folder-as-agent pattern in one respect: both are about accumulating a personalized environment over time that compounds in effectiveness. Counter-arguments High upfront learning curve and configuration cost Tool overlap creates maintenance burden Some users may get equivalent results with fewer, more integrated tools (e.g., Raycast replacing LaunchBar + clipboard + snippets) Data gaps No structured comparison of this stack vs simpler alternatives Cost and conflict information missing Single source

title: Commonplace Practice
text: Commonplace Practice A daily habit of jotting down observations, epiphanies, thoughts, and questions. Rooted in the historical tradition of commonplace books but adapted for LLM-augmented knowledge work. In the context of a Claude + Obsidian workflow, the practice serves a dual purpose: personal reflection and building context for AI agents. The more raw personal material captured, the more an LLM can identify patterns and make connections. Key principles (per YB): Unstructured is fine - optimize for capture speed, not formatting Voice transcription preferred - captures more including uncertainty markers Context compounds - claimed benefits emerge after weeks of consistent practice Personal, not synthetic - distinguishes this from llm-wiki-pattern research workflows where AI generates the artifacts Counter-arguments Unstructured notes may produce noise that degrades LLM context quality The compounding claim rests on a single practitioner's report Historical commonplace books worked without AI - unclear what the LLM integration adds beyond search Data gaps No metrics on how much daily capture is needed for the compounding effect No comparison with structured journaling approaches Single source - needs corroboration

title: Compound Engineering
text: Compound Engineering An AI-native engineering philosophy created by Kieran Klaassen at Every.to. The core idea: context accumulated in project folders over months of work makes each subsequent AI interaction more effective. The folder-as-agent pattern is a direct product of this approach. Has 14,000+ stars on GitHub. Every.to offers a compound engineering guide and a plugin. Efficiency Counterpart: Context Compression As compound engineering accumulates context, token costs grow. Context Compression tools address this: Caveman Compress compresses the files themselves (~46% reduction), while Claude-Mem adds progressive disclosure to avoid loading everything (~10x savings). These are the efficiency layer that makes sustained compound engineering economically viable. Counter-arguments "Accumulated context improves results" is intuitive but hard to measure May conflate project maturity with compound engineering benefits Data gaps Source article mentions compound engineering but doesn't explain the methodology in detail The GitHub guide and plugin not yet ingested as primary sources No comparison with non-compound approaches on the same project

title: Knowledge Work PRs
text: Knowledge Work PRs A workflow pattern where LLM agent subroutines process vault content and propose changes that the human reviews, analogous to code pull requests. Described by YB (yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice) as the culmination of the Claude + Obsidian setup, but only effective if the preceding practices (commonplace-practice, atomic-notes, intentional-reading, agentic-constitution) are in place. How it works User defines subroutines (instruction sets) in the vault Each subroutine starts by reading the agentic-constitution Agent executes the subroutine and drafts changes User reviews changes one-by-one in a voice session (approve/tweak/reject) Approved changes get committed to the vault Example: "Tend the Vault" - checks daily commonplace-practice entries since last run, identifies threads and insights, drafts updates to atomic-notes, presents changes for review. The metaphor: agents are developers, you are the engineering manager reviewing PRs before they hit production. Relationship to Folder-as-Agent Kieran Klaassen's folder-as-agent pattern describes a similar human-in-the-loop dynamic at the engineering level: parallel Claude Code sessions work when each agent has a clear task and a human directs the work, but agent swarms coordinating autonomously fail. Knowledge Work PRs formalize this same constraint for personal knowledge management - the agent proposes, the human disposes. Counter-arguments The PR metaphor may break down - knowledge work changes are harder

title: Atomic Notes
text: Atomic Notes A note-taking practice where each note captures a single concept rather than facts, names, or dates. Attributed to Andy Matuschak's evergreen notes practice, itself building on Niklas Luhmann's Zettelkasten method. YB (yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice) frames atomic notes as "API endpoints you call when solving problems" - reusable conceptual building blocks that can be combined across life domains you'd normally keep separate. In an LLM-augmented vault, atomic notes serve as the structured layer that makes commonplace-practice captures useful. Once enough atomic notes exist, you can dictate a train of thought and the AI pulls relevant past thinking from across the vault. Connects to Paul Graham's observation that "writing is thinking" - the act of distilling a concept into an atomic note forces clarity. Counter-arguments Atomicity is subjective - practitioners disagree on the right granularity The "API endpoint" metaphor may overstate how cleanly concepts decompose Overhead of maintaining many small notes can become its own burden Data gaps Matuschak's original writing not yet ingested as a primary source No evidence on optimal note count before LLM recall becomes useful Single source describing the practice in an LLM context

title: Agentic Constitution
text: Agentic Constitution A file that serves as the bridge between an Obsidian vault and Claude (or other LLM agents). Contains high-level personal context: who you are, your big question, how you like to work, main goals, current problems, team context. Described by YB (yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice) as the first file agents read before doing any work, ensuring alignment with the user's current priorities and thinking style. Key characteristics: Living document - updated weekly as priorities shift Team-like communication - treat it as briefing a team member, not configuring software Portal function - the primary interface between personal knowledge and AI agents Feeds subroutines - all knowledge-work-prs start by reading this file Functionally similar to a project instruction file, but scoped to personal identity and goals rather than codebase conventions. Relationship to Folder-as-Agent Kieran Klaassen (klaassen-2026-folder-is-the-agent) describes a related pattern at the project level: the CLAUDE.md file in a project folder, combined with accumulated documentation and operational memory, turns the folder itself into an agent. Where the agentic constitution captures personal-level context (identity, goals, work style), the folder-as-agent captures project-level context (code conventions, architecture, runbooks). Both converge on the principle that accumulated context is the agent. Claude Cowork "Brain File" JJ Englert (englert-2026-claude-cowork-tutorial) demonstrates the

title: Folder-as-Agent Pattern
text: Folder-as-Agent Pattern A design pattern where a project folder - containing instruction files (CLAUDE.md/AGENT.md), skill definitions, documentation, and accumulated operational context - transforms a generalized LLM into a specialist agent. Coined by Kieran Klaassen in the context of building Cora at Every.to. Core claim: An agent is a model with enough context so you don't have to re-explain everything each time you open the chat. The folder provides that context. The same model pointed at different folders becomes a fundamentally different agent. Layers of a Folder-Agent Conventions - coding standards, deploy workflows, naming patterns (CLAUDE.md) Institutional knowledge - architecture docs, design decisions, domain documentation Operational memory - runbooks, incident investigations, resolved bugs Specialized sub-agents - refined instruction files for specific tasks (reviewers, planners) Relationship to Other Patterns Agentic Constitution - similar principle applied at the personal level rather than project level. The constitution captures identity and goals; the folder captures project knowledge. LLM Wiki Pattern - another form of pre-compiled context, but organized as a queryable knowledge base rather than an agent instruction set. Compound Engineering - Klaassen's broader methodology; the folder-as-agent pattern is one outcome of sustained compound engineering practice. Claude Cowork as Folder-Agent UI JJ Englert (englert-2026-claude-cowork-tutorial) demonstrates the

title: YB - Build a Daily Commonplace Practice
text: YB - Build a Daily Commonplace Practice Source: Tweet thread by YB (@yb_effect), 2026-04-13 Engagement: 129 likes, 279 bookmarks, 6.1K impressions !yb-effect-commonplace-practice-overview.jpg The image summarizes the five practices and recommended Obsidian plugins (Copilot, Dataview, Readwise, Excalidraw, Simple Note Review). Summary YB describes a Claude + Obsidian workflow they've used for 9+ months, arguing it fundamentally changed their daily habits. The thread distinguishes this personal thinking use case from andrej-karpathy's llm-wiki-pattern, which YB considers better suited for research projects with synthetic artifacts. The key difference: for personal thinking, you can't outsource your own life experiences to AI - the personal context you add is what makes the tool useful. Five Practices Commonplace Practice Daily habit of jotting down observations, epiphanies, thoughts, and questions. Notes don't need to be clean or structured. Voice transcription recommended because it captures more context, including uncertainty ("ifs, buts, and ums"). The claim: context compounds over weeks and months, producing surprising pattern recognition. Atomic Notes (via Andy Matuschak) Notes should be concepts, not facts/names/dates. YB frames them as "API endpoints you call when solving problems." Thinking in atomic notes lets you pull context from different life domains you'd normally keep separate. References Paul Graham's "writing is thinking" - write notes for yourself, not for an

title: Alex Newman - Claude-Mem
text: Alex Newman - Claude-Mem Source: GitHub README Author: Alex Newman (@thedotmack) Format: Open-source Claude Code plugin License: AGPL-3.0 Summary Claude-Mem is a persistent memory compression system for Claude Code that captures what Claude learns during tool interactions, compresses observations into semantic summaries, and makes them available to future sessions. It addresses context-compression through progressive disclosure rather than file compression. Architecture Lifecycle hooks tie into Claude Code events: SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse, Stop, SessionEnd Worker service runs on port 37777 with HTTP API and web viewer UI (Bun runtime) Storage uses SQLite with FTS5 full-text search plus Chroma vector database for hybrid semantic/keyword search 3-Layer Progressive Disclosure The key design: instead of dumping all context at once, retrieval happens in three layers with ~10x token savings: search - compact index with IDs (~50-100 tokens per result) timeline - chronological context around observations get_observations - full details for filtered IDs (~500-1,000 tokens per result) This lets Claude selectively load only the context it needs rather than consuming tokens on irrelevant history. Relationship to Other Tools Solves the same problem as Caveman Compress (reducing token costs for persistent context) but from the opposite direction: claude-mem adds a retrieval layer so you don't load everything, while caveman compresses the files themselves. Both are contex

title: Kieran Klaassen - The Folder Is the Agent
text: Kieran Klaassen - The Folder Is the Agent Source: Article by Kieran Klaassen, Every.to, 2026-04-13 Context: Klaassen is general manager of Cora (Every's AI email assistant) and creator of the compound-engineering methodology (14,000+ GitHub stars). Summary Klaassen spent three months trying to make agent swarms work - multiple AI agents coordinating with each other, deciding what to work on, producing output he hadn't shaped. The experiment failed: when 10 agents finished simultaneously, he had 10 results to evaluate without enough context to trust any of them. The insight: "AI agents don't have a speed limit, but the person managing them still does." He stopped looking for a smarter orchestration layer and realized the real agent was something he already had - a project folder. A folder with a CLAUDE.md/AGENT.md file, skill definitions, and context accumulated through months of compound-engineering makes a generalized model into a specialist. The Folder-as-Agent Pattern An agent is defined as: a model with enough context so you don't have to re-explain everything each time you open the chat. The ~/cora/ folder demonstrates the layers: Conventions and standards - CLAUDE.md covers Rails conventions, deploy workflows, database patterns Institutional knowledge - docs/developer-docs/ holds architecture reports, email processing pipeline, assistant system design Operational memory - docs/runbooks/ and docs/investigations/ capture patterns from real incidents Specialized agents - .

title: Brett Terpstra - Keyboard-Driven Mac Utility List
text: Brett Terpstra - Keyboard-Driven Mac Utility List Source: Blog post by Brett Terpstra, 2026-03-27 Context: Terpstra is a developer and writer focused on macOS productivity tooling. Summary A curated overview of 20 macOS utilities organized around the principle that the keyboard is the most powerful input tool on a Mac. Terpstra uses all of these daily and describes how they complement each other. Categories and Tools Keyboard Navigation Superkey - searches text on screen, simulates mouse click on matching text. Navigate without leaving keyboard. Wooshy - alternative to Superkey for navigating all UI elements via keyboard. KindaVim - brings Vim-like key bindings (h/j/k/l/u/d/a/i) to any macOS app. Not perfect but great for basic Vim navigation. Paletro - Shift+Cmd+P in any app for fuzzy menu item search. Used daily. Scrolla - keyboard-driven scrolling through documents. Keyboard Modifications BetterTouchTool - trackpad gesture and keyboard shortcut manager. Creates personalized commands. Karabiner-Elements - system-level key remapping. Terpstra uses it for Hyper key setup. DefaultKeyBindings.dict - macOS built-in key mapping file for custom keyboard shortcuts. Keyboard Maestro - automation engine for repetitive tasks. Combined with key bindings for streamlined text editing. Launchers LaunchBar - Terpstra's preferred launcher after trying all alternatives. Features: clipboard history across reboots, Instant Send (double-tap Option), file actions. Combined with KindaVim and Supe

title: llm-wikid README - AI-Maintained Knowledge Base for Obsidian
text: llm-wikid README - AI-Maintained Knowledge Base for Obsidian !llm-wikid-cover.png Summary llm-wikid is an open-source project by shann-holmberg that implements andrej-karpathy's llm-wiki-pattern as a ready-to-clone Obsidian vault. The core idea: you dump raw sources (tweets, articles, PDFs, ideas) into a folder, and an AI agent compiles them into structured, cross-referenced wiki pages in . Key Claims This is not RAG. The system explicitly distinguishes itself from retrieval-augmented generation. RAG re-derives answers every time by chunking documents and running vector search. This system pre-compiles knowledge into structured wiki pages, keeps them current, and pre-builds cross-references. At ~100 articles / ~400K words, Karpathy found this outperforms RAG for Q&A. The compound loop. Every query answer gets filed back into the wiki, so the next query has more to work with. The system grows smarter with use, not just with new source material. Agent-agnostic. Works with any agent that can read markdown and run shell commands - Claude Code, OpenClaw, Hermes, Codex, or custom setups. Ingest Pipeline The system processes sources in phases: Sort clippings - is an inbox. Files get moved to the right subfolder (tweets to bookmarks, articles to articles, PDFs to papers) Resolve - URLs get fetched via the appropriate tool (yt-dlp for YouTube, scrapling for web, X API for tweets) Media extraction - Images downloaded and analyzed, video transcripts extracted Classify and compile - Type

title: Julius Brussee - Caveman Compress
text: Julius Brussee - Caveman Compress Source: GitHub README Author: Julius Brussee Format: Open-source Claude Code skill Summary Caveman Compress is a Claude Code skill that reduces token consumption by compressing project memory files (CLAUDE.md, todos, preferences) into a more concise format while preserving their semantic content and structure. Part of a broader pattern of context-compression tools that reduce the cost of persistent context in LLM workflows. How It Works File type detection (local, no API call) Claude-powered compression (single API call to rewrite concisely) Validation (local checks that headings, code blocks, URLs, file paths, bullets survive) Targeted repairs (patches only broken sections, no full recompression) Output generation (compressed version + human-readable backup) Preservation Rules Code blocks, inline code, URLs, file paths, technical terms, heading text, table structure, and numeric values are never modified. Only natural language prose gets compressed. Benchmarks ~46% average token reduction across five file types: Preference files: 59.6% Project notes: 53.3% Project metadata: 43.3% Todo lists: 38.1% Mixed documentation: 36.9% Real example: 706-token preferences file → 285 tokens, identical instructions. Relationship to Other Tools Addresses the same problem as Claude-Mem (reducing token costs for persistent context) but from the opposite direction: caveman compresses the files themselves, while claude-mem provides progressive disclosure to avo

title: JJ Englert - Claude Cowork Tutorial for Non-Engineers
text: JJ Englert - Claude Cowork Tutorial for Non-Engineers Source: How I AI podcast (YouTube), hosted by Claire Vo Author: JJ Englert (community enablement at Tenex) Date: 2026-04-13 Format: Video tutorial (~50 min) with transcript Summary A comprehensive walkthrough of Claude Cowork, Anthropic's desktop tool positioned between simple chat and terminal-based Claude Code. Aimed at non-engineers who want AI to do work, not just suggest it. Core Patterns Brain file strategy - Create a preferences document that Claude reads at the start of every session. Contains who you are, how you work, communication style, and priorities. Functionally identical to an agentic-constitution, applied within Cowork's project structure. Projects as folders - Each Cowork project maps to a folder on your computer. Shared memory persists across conversations within a project, building context over time. Reinforces the folder-as-agent pattern: the project folder accumulates context that makes Claude more effective with each interaction. One-click connectors - Gmail, Slack, Notion, Google Calendar connect with a single click. Permission controls let you set Claude to draft-only (never send) or always-ask mode. The connectors transform Claude from advisor ("here's what you should do") to executor ("I did it for you"). Sub-advisory board - Spin up three AI agents with different personas (e.g., your boss, your engineering partner, your customer) to review work from multiple perspectives before you present it. E

title: Karabiner-Elements
text: Karabiner-Elements Open-source macOS tool for system-level key remapping and behavior modification. Used by Brett Terpstra primarily for Hyper key setup, which unlocks additional keyboard shortcut possibilities. Website: karabiner-elements.pqrs.org. Part of the keyboard modification layer in a keyboard-driven-workflow, alongside BetterTouchTool, Keyboard Maestro, and DefaultKeyBindings.dict.

title: Caveman Compress
text: Caveman Compress Open-source Claude Code skill by Julius Brussee that reduces token consumption by compressing project memory files (CLAUDE.md, todos, preferences) while preserving semantic content. Achieves ~46% average token reduction. Uses a pipeline: file detection → Claude-powered compression → validation → targeted repairs. Preserves code blocks, URLs, file paths, and structural elements. Part of the context-compression landscape. Complements Claude-Mem (which uses progressive disclosure instead of file compression). Command: . Requires Python 3.10+.

title: JJ Englert
text: JJ Englert Community enablement lead at Tenex. Known for a widely-shared article asking "Who is Cowork for anyway?" and a subsequent zero-to-one Claude Cowork tutorial on the How I AI podcast. Practices: brain files for agent context (agentic-constitution), sub-advisory boards with multiple AI personas, scheduled morning debriefs, progressive trust model for AI permissions. Uses Cowork for productivity (email, Slack, documents) and Claude Code for building.

title: llm-wikid
text: llm-wikid Open-source project by shann-holmberg that implements andrej-karpathy's llm-wiki-pattern as a ready-to-clone obsidian vault. Agent-agnostic - works with Claude Code, OpenClaw, Hermes, Codex, or custom setups. Core operations: INGEST (process raw sources), QUERY (research and file answers), EXPLORE (expand topics), LINT (health check). Uses qmd for local search at scale. Stub - expand when more sources reference this entity.

title: Kagi
text: Kagi Paid search engine offering full keyboard navigation of results and AI slop filtering. Used by Brett Terpstra alongside Vimium for a keyboard-driven web browsing experience. Website: kagi.com.

title: Tobi Lutke
text: Tobi Lutke Creator of qmd, a local markdown search engine with hybrid BM25/vector search. CEO of Shopify. Stub - expand when more sources reference this entity.

title: Claude-Mem
text: Claude-Mem Open-source persistent memory system for Claude Code by Alex Newman (@thedotmack). Captures observations during tool interactions, compresses them into semantic summaries, and makes them available across sessions. Architecture: lifecycle hooks → SQLite (FTS5) + Chroma vector DB → 3-layer progressive disclosure (search → timeline → full details). Claims ~10x token savings vs loading all context. Part of the context-compression landscape. Complements Caveman Compress (which compresses files directly instead of adding a retrieval layer). Supports Claude Code, Gemini CLI, and OpenClaw. Licensed AGPL-3.0.

title: Shann Holmberg
text: Shann Holmberg Creator of llm-wikid. Known on X as @shannholmberg and on GitHub as shannhk. Stub - expand when more sources reference this entity.

title: Obsidian
text: Obsidian Markdown-based knowledge management application. In the context of llm-wikid, serves as the UI layer for browsing the wiki - graph view for visualizing connections, Dataview for structured queries, and Web Clipper for collecting sources from the browser into . Recommended plugins (from llm-wikid): Dataview, Obsidian Git, Templater, Tag Wrangler. Additional plugins recommended by YB (yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice): Copilot, Readwise, Excalidraw, Simple Note Review. As a Personal Thinking Tool YB describes using Obsidian + Claude as a personal thinking system built around five practices: commonplace-practice, atomic-notes, intentional-reading, agentic-constitution, and knowledge-work-prs. This use case differs from the llm-wiki-pattern research workflow - the emphasis is on capturing personal context that AI can't generate on its own.

title: Paul Graham
text: Paul Graham Essayist, programmer, and co-founder of Y Combinator. Referenced by YB (yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice) for the idea that "writing is thinking" - used to motivate the practice of writing atomic-notes for oneself as a way of developing thought. Stub - expand when more sources reference this entity.

title: Readwise
text: Readwise Reading highlights aggregation tool. Captures highlights from books, articles, podcasts, and other sources. Includes a Reader app for focused reading with annotation. The Obsidian plugin syncs highlights into the vault, enabling users to connect reading material to atomic-notes and other knowledge. Central to the intentional-reading pipeline described by YB (yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice). Stub - expand when more sources reference this entity.

title: Brett Terpstra
text: Brett Terpstra Developer and writer based in Minnesota, USA. Maintains a blog at brettterpstra.com focused on macOS productivity tools and automation. Active on GitHub (ttscoff) and Mastodon. Notable projects: NiftyMenu (menu item scanner), Cheaters (cheatsheet viewer), custom DefaultKeyBindings.dict for macOS, Karabiner-Elements mods. Known for his extensive keyboard-driven-workflow combining 20+ macOS utilities. Key tools in his daily stack: LaunchBar, Keyboard Maestro, Karabiner-Elements, BetterTouchTool, KindaVim, Superkey, Moom, Vimium, Cotypist.

title: Andy Matuschak
text: Andy Matuschak Researcher and designer known for work on tools for thought. Former Apple (iOS) and Khan Academy. Creator of the evergreen notes / atomic-notes practice, which emphasizes writing concept-oriented notes that build on each other over time. Referenced by YB (yb-effect-2026-commonplace-practice) as the foundational influence for atomic note-taking in a Claude + Obsidian workflow. Stub - expand when more sources reference this entity.

title: Kieran Klaassen
text: Kieran Klaassen General manager of Cora, Every.to's AI email assistant. Creator of compound-engineering, an AI-native engineering philosophy with 14,000+ GitHub stars. Sole engineer building Cora. Known for the folder-as-agent pattern - the claim that a project folder with accumulated context is the real "agent," not the model. Runs 44 such folder-agents with a custom dispatch layer. Primarily uses Claude Opus 4.6, also GPT 5.4 and Gemini Pro 3.1. Published at Every.to's Source Code column. Collaborates with product leader Trevin Chow on compound engineering workshops.

title: BetterTouchTool
text: BetterTouchTool macOS application for trackpad gesture management and keyboard shortcut customization. Used by Brett Terpstra in his keyboard-driven-workflow. Website: folivora.ai. Part of the keyboard modification layer alongside Karabiner-Elements and Keyboard Maestro.

title: Keyboard Maestro
text: Keyboard Maestro macOS automation application for creating keyboard-triggered macros and complex workflows. Used by Brett Terpstra as part of his keyboard-driven-workflow for automating repetitive tasks and streamlining text editing. Website: keyboardmaestro.com. Combined with Karabiner-Elements for key remapping and DefaultKeyBindings.dict for system-level shortcuts, forms the modification layer of a keyboard-driven setup.

title: LaunchBar
text: LaunchBar macOS application launcher and productivity tool by Objective Development. Features: clipboard history across reboots, Instant Send (double-tap Option key), file actions, quick calculations. Brett Terpstra's preferred launcher. Website: obdev.at/launchbar. Combined with KindaVim for Finder navigation and Superkey for screen text selection, forms a powerful mouse-free navigation system.

title: Cora
text: Cora AI email assistant built by Every.to. Developed by Kieran Klaassen as sole engineer. The primary case study for the folder-as-agent pattern and compound-engineering methodology. Website: cora.computer.

title: Claude Cowork
text: Claude Cowork Anthropic's desktop productivity tool, positioned between simple chat and terminal-based Claude Code. Available in the Claude desktop app as the middle tab (Chat | Cowork | Code). Designed for non-engineers who want AI to execute tasks, not just advise. Key features: Folder-based projects with shared memory across conversations (reinforces folder-as-agent) One-click connectors to Gmail, Slack, Notion, Google Calendar with granular permissions Skills - reusable instructions for specific tasks (writing style, advisory boards, research) Scheduled tasks - recurring automations (e.g., morning debrief at 7:30 a.m.) Sub-agents - multiple AI personas for multi-perspective review The "brain file" pattern (a preferences document Claude reads every session) is functionally identical to an agentic-constitution. Counter-arguments May be a UI wrapper that adds friction vs direct Claude Code usage Connector quality determines the ceiling of the tool Data gaps Single tutorial source - no comparative reviews or benchmarks Cost/token implications of scheduled tasks and sub-agents unclear

title: Vimium
text: Vimium Browser extension that adds Vim-style navigation to web pages. Used by Brett Terpstra in his keyboard-driven-workflow. Key feature: pressing F tags every visible link with key sequences for keyboard-only navigation. Website: vimium.github.io.

title: qmd
text: qmd Local markdown search engine by tobi-lutke. Three search modes: BM25 keyword (fast), vector semantic, and hybrid with LLM re-ranking (best results). Runs 100% on-device. Built-in MCP server for agent integration. Used by llm-wikid as the search layer when wiki scales past ~300 pages. Stub - expand when more sources reference this entity.

title: Andrej Karpathy
text: Andrej Karpathy AI researcher known for work on computer vision and neural networks. Former director of AI at Tesla, founding member of OpenAI. Originated the llm-wiki-pattern for personal knowledge management, reporting that at ~100 articles it outperforms RAG for Q&A. Stub - expand when more sources reference this entity.

title: LLM Wikid
text: LLM Wikid An AI-maintained knowledge base that lives in Obsidian. Based on Karpathy's LLM Wiki pattern. You dump raw sources into a folder. An AI agent reads them, compiles structured wiki pages with cross-references, runs bias checks, and maintains a master index. Every question you ask gets filed back in. The wiki compounds the more you use it. Works with any agent that can read markdown and run shell commands: Claude Code, OpenClaw, Hermes, Codex, or your own setup. Quick Start ```CLAUDE.md``/wiki-ingestraw/raw/clippings/raw/bookmarks/raw/articles/raw/papers/yt-dlpscraplingraw/assets/images/wikilinksqmd/wiki-ingest/wiki-query [question]/wiki-explore [topic]/last30days/wiki-lint``## Counter-arguments## Data gapsexplored: falsetruehighmediumlowuncertainwikilinkraw//wiki-lintwiki/raw/git revert``/wiki-ingest````````/wiki-explore/wiki-exploreraw/clippings/yt-dlpbrew install yt-dlpscraplingpipx install scraplingsummarizebrew install steipete/tap/summarize.env` (optional) | Credits Andrej Karpathy's LLM Wiki pattern - the original idea hooeem's LLM Knowledge Base course - practical walkthrough qmd by Tobi Lutke - local markdown search engine last30days by mvanhorn - multi-platform signal search Built by @shannholmberg

title: claude
text: # LLM Knowledge Base - Schema Overview Personal knowledge base maintained by an LLM agent. Raw sources live in . The compiled wiki lives in . You (the AI) maintain all wiki content. The user directs strategy and source collection; you execute compilation, maintenance, and queries. This is NOT RAG. Knowledge is pre-compiled into wiki pages. The agent reads structured pages, not raw chunks. Every query compounds the system. Directory Structure Wiki - Master index linking every page with TLDR for fast scanning - Append-only changelog of all operations - Concept pages (ideas, frameworks, topics) - Entity pages (people, companies, tools, protocols) - Source summary pages (one per raw source) - Cross-cutting analysis across sources - Filed answers to queries - Repeatable processes and workflows - Starter templates per page type (for Obsidian Templater) Raw - Idea notes and brainstorms - Bookmarked tweet and article sources - X archive tweets (exported from X/Twitter) - Obsidian Web Clipper inbox. Everything lands here. During INGEST, files get sorted to the correct subfolder and removed from clippings. - Long-form articles and blog posts (sorted from clippings) - Research papers and PDFs (sorted from clippings) - Images, videos, and other media New wiki pages go in the subfolder matching their frontmatter field. Wikilinks use filename only (Obsidian resolves via shortest-path setting). Frontmatter Schema Every wiki page MUST have YAML frontmatter: ```exploredtrueexplored: trueconfi
